transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi.
Hi, how are you?
I'm good.
How are you, Russell?
I'm pretty well, thank you.
I hope this wasn't a last-minute change, because I know you guys were out yesterday, and this is 9 a.m.
for you, so.
Oh, I mean, 9's, 9's pretty chill.
Okay, cool.
Well, for me personally, it's a piece of cake, but otherwise, yeah, all good.
Hi, Annie.
Hello.
I'm just checking your Slack message, Annie.
Yeah, I just wanted to update you.

We're over to you shortly as well.
Okay, great.
Thank you.
Yeah, that's just really quickly on the sort of analytics and access front.
When you were giving us access to Google Search Console, do you remember what level of access we were given?
I don't remember off the top of my head, but I'm happy to double check.

Yeah, because it would be helpful for us because you guys implemented that fix to resolve the canonicalization issue, but we need to manually submit some pages for indexing, and right now we don't have permissions to do that.
Typically, we do.
So, you know, we don't want to bother you with that stuff, so it's easier if we can just do it.
Cool, I'll check your permissions right now and grant you additional access.
Okay, sounds good.
Hi.
Hi.
Devon Good morning.
How doing, man?
Better.
Almost everyone is not sick anymore, except for Tiffany.
Go, progress.
Yeah, there was a, I feel like it was a cross-continental, like, flu that was going around these past couple of weeks.
It was crazy.
Right.
Yeah, last week was rough.
My 15-month-old had to go to the ER with the flu.
Oh, no.
She's okay, though.
Don't worry.
Yeah, no, that's, yeah, that sucks.

We have a couple of team members who have young kids as well, and it's always just, it's, they always, like, you know, if the kindergarten sends them home, then they have to go and, like, reschedule a bunch of stuff.
So it's not easy, I feel for you.
All right.
I think Nikki might join in a little bit.
You're right.
but
I think we can go ahead and get started.
Not a ton of updates considering we saw each other on Thursday, but I'm happy to hear that you guys are continuing to phase two.
If you have any additional questions, feel free to ask.
But we have like the main takeaway is that we have the foundational strategy in place.
We have the content OS we're publishing.
The review process has been very, very smooth.
I have to say, like the team, Nikki and Autran and the rest of the people from the product team, they've been really good at just reviewing things super quickly.
And we don't have, I don't think we have anything at the backlog right now, if I'm looking.
Yeah, like these two are still being reviewed by Nikki, but you know, you've been good at like just pretty much reviewing everything super fast.
So that has allowed us to publish really quickly.
And we've published.
published 17 articles so far, and I think we're on track to just continue delivering five, publishing five every week, which is where we want to be.
You know, one quick question on that.
How is the review cycle then spinning back into artifacts and approach?
Like, I just want to, because they are doing a good job staying on top of it, but it is a lot right now.
Like, the amount of review and sort of how carefully we have to look at every single article.
When we build up a backlog, it is quite a bit of work for the team to work through.
So, I guess I'm looking for, like, some details there, and then also just reassurance that, like, the level of review probably decreases over time somewhat.
Can you speak to that at all?
Yeah, absolutely.
No, so every time a piece of content goes through review, any piece of feedback that applies across multiple pieces of content, we go and update them.
The appropriate artifacts, so whether it's the company, it's usually the company context or the writing guidelines.
Those are the two that end up being updated the most.
I think it's very important for us to have visibility into how the product team is reviewing.

So if there are any things that we could do to make it easier for them, perhaps like in the next couple of weeks, once they start getting comfortable with the content and we get to a level where they don't have to read the whole article, we could just highlight any Cresta mentions and then they could just zero in on those sections so that they spend less time reviewing.
But the goal is to get to a point where the feedback isn't as extensive as it is right now.
But we do update them every, like after every review cycle, every week, we end up updating them.
I think a couple of weeks ago.
You provided the buyer's guides, and I think we got a new PDF last week.
There was a slight delay in content production because we wanted to update everything that we're using before we deliver you the new batch of content.
So it's on our mind constantly.
Okay, cool.
That makes sense.

I think some of where I'm starting to see a heavier review required is actually like in the less so in the like Cresta specifics, but more so in, I guess, like category or like topics related to like content center functions.
So like the QM article, I think required a lot of work and input from Orin and a bunch of that was, I think, just like web research that your workflow did to pull in the content there.
So I just want to make sure that that also is not just like going into the ether after an article like that.
think all the work that he put into that and then the...
The work your team put into folding in those revisions, we can maybe create like a QM artifact or something like that.
That's about like QM programs and sort of how we speak to them in general.
That's maybe a little bit beyond what's in the other artifacts.

And I think same for like conversation intelligence, conversation analytics, VOC, just the way that we've like workshopped the general language and descriptions of like those functions and categories, I think is worthwhile.
So maybe that's already happening, but if not, we should make sure that it is, because I think we're going to continue to tackle those topics with different sort of wedges and angles.
And so it'll be helpful to build that up as we do reviews.
Yeah, absolutely.
I think I'm actually meeting the team in an hour to discuss like the editing process for Cresta content and like all the feedback that we've been getting.
So this is perfect timing.
There are a couple of things that we could do here.
I think, and I'll consult with the team to see.

What the best approach is, sometimes it's about constraining the researcher because a lot of the information that appears in the draft is a result of our researcher taking the article direction and then generating questions that it has to answer in order to compile information to then create an article.
However, if those questions are conflating very nuanced concepts, then that's going to also be reflected in the draft.

For example, one of our fintech clients, we recently had a situation where the researcher was pulling information about credit card fraud, but the researcher did not specify business credit card fraud.
Like, we don't care about credit card fraud.
Like, it's important, but it's not relevant for our clients.
So there are just some tweaks that we have to make to constrain that workflow.
But I will talk to them about...

It's not, you know, with every client that we work with, I would say about like 50 or 60% of the time the product team is involved and whenever they are involved, we have to like take additional steps to really make sure that like the feedback isn't repetitive.
So I think any of those themes, I will talk to them about that today and see what we can do.
Makes sense.

Yeah, mean, and in your defense, in the defense of the process, you know, that we didn't have very good documentation of like tactical level scorecard building related stuff outside of like more philosophical positioning related stuff in some of our decks.
So I don't think like the input that the research agent was able to get from us was probably high enough quality.
And then so it seeks that outer context and then ends up with funky stuff in the article.
But anyway, I think that's going to happen regardless with how fast we're trying to move.
It's just wanting to make sure that we like preserve the work that we do do to correct it.
For any future uses.
So that all makes sense to me.
Appreciate it.
Got it.
No worries.
All right.
And then for the content that's coming up in the next couple of weeks.
So we do have 13.

Hold on.
Let me check.

And Devon, I remember this was a few weeks back.

So those assignments.
Are an ideas for review.
So I think what would be helpful for us is, you know, you could just like have a skim and let me know in the external channel if, you know, we're on the right track.
And then I can come up with a prioritization framework for like the next two months.
So we kind of have a clear plan for what we want to target and when, depending on like the goals that we set out in our initial strategy.
Okay, that works.
Yeah, I can run through those and give you sort of a similar level of feedback as the first set.
Yeah, yeah, that works.
And yeah, you can leave, you know, you can leave feedback as like comments here.
Or if you go to the topic clusters, you can do that same thing here.

Okay.
One question for you.
So Okay.
We did that, like, deep dive walkthrough and demo.
I'm blanking on his name for one of the other members of the team.
probably?

For some of the areas where we don't have, like, a lot of long-form written content explaining it all, but we do have, like, decks and positioning and talk tracks, would that help if he, you know, spent an hour and just did a briefing on coaching?
Something like that, or a briefing on QM?
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
Sometime over the next couple of weeks.
Okay.

More of his time to save four to five hours of his time over the next few months for viewing.

And we can just like run him through like we can just show him the titles on the call and he could like call out any like unique angles or anything that we should, you know, make sure to add.
Yeah.
Okay, that makes sense.
Okay, sounds good.
All right.
So we have a plan for the new topic approvals.
Once Annie, like increases our access permissions, I'll go in and just submit all published guides for indexing.
Last week, Russell, we were talking a little bit about how some of the technical issues on your site might have impacted your website.
right.
right.
So I went into your Google Search Console and I exported all of the different URLs that have been discovered but not indexed.
So it means that Google's bots did find them, but they did not index them for different reasons.
They don't provide a specific reason, but it's usually because there's a technical SEO issue on the site.
And I exported that doc here and a lot of these are just like author pages, which aren't as important, but you can find that there's a lot of blogs here that aren't indexed.
And that's just for you to kind of be aware, like, you know, I'm sure that these were published over like a period of time.

Also news and press, and some webinars.
Okay.
Thank you for going through it.
Yeah, no worries.

I imagine we just turned on the flag for...

forward.

Next week.
You will meet your new engagement manager for phase two.

And yeah, we're meeting this week to kind of discuss everything that has to do with Cresta and what we've done over the past weeks.
And she also has access to all the documentation that we've created.
So we'll be working together pretty heavily this week just to make sure that she has all the context.
And yeah, any questions come up in the meantime, just ping me.
Cool.
Why do you structure it that way?
I'm curious.
That's a good question.
So we have like the sprint team is there to make sure that we have like a specific way of working.
We know how to extract like key information from clients.
And we've found that having the same team working.
Working on a repeatable process gets better outcomes rather than a new client comes in, they're assigned to a team, and they are just with that team from the beginning.
We have like a process that we've kind of been perfecting over the past couple of months.
And sometimes it turns out that a client isn't like a good fit for us or we're not going to fit for the client.
So it's just easier to not disturb the rest of the delivery team if that's not the case.
Got it.
Okay, cool.
All right.
I think those are all the updates that we had.
Is there anything else that you wanted to discuss?
All good.
All good.
All right.
Thank you.
I will send a follow-up in Slack and yeah, we'll be in touch.
I will see you all next week.
Thank you.
Thank you.
